text: The patient is positioned in the Leo Davis position with a fifteen-degree left tilt, causing the small intestine to visibly shift to the left side of the abdominal cavity.
The assistant uses an intestinal forcep to lift the terminal ileum, positioning it for better access within the surgical field.

The surgeon separates the attachment of the terminal ileum from the posterior peritoneum, exposing the peritoneum of the ileocecal region on the lateral side and the superior mesenteric artery trunk on the medial side.
The assistant lifts the terminal ileum using an intestinal forcep, maintaining exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon enters the surgical field through the incision, navigating the space between the toes gap.
The surgeon separates the mesocolon of the right hemicolon from the surface of the duodenum and pancreas head using both blunt and sharp dissection techniques, progressing towards the duodenal bulb.
The patient is positioned in a dorsal elevated position at a twenty-degree angle and tilted fifteen degrees to the left, exposing the gastric colon ligament for further surgical access.

The assistant elevates the greater curvature of the stomach, allowing the surgeon to open the gastrocolic ligament at the middle of the transverse colon. The hepatic flexure of the colon is then separated up to the border of the lateral peritoneum.
The surgeon places gauze at the foot of the anterior lobe of the transverse mesocolon, marking the end of the transverse mesocolon for the next step.
The surgeon makes a horizontal incision at the root of the transverse mesocolon, using it as a guideline to identify the anatomical endpoint of the superior mesenteric vein.
The assistant elevates the mesocolon to create a forty-five degree angle with the posterior peritoneum, then incises the avascular zone of the ileocolic mesentery below the ileocolic vessel.
The surgeon incises the mesoilium along the trunk of the superior mesenteric vein, aiding in the identification of the superior mesenteric vein.
The surgeon exposes the root of the ileocolic fascia from the right side of the superior mesenteric vein, revealing the anatomical structures in this area.
The surgeon ligates and incises the root of the ileocolic vein, completing the dissection at this anatomical site.
The surgeon ligates and incises the root of the ileocolic artery, following the anatomical guidelines established by previous incisions near the superior mesenteric vein.
The surgical field reveals the absence of the right colonic vein in the patient, as the surgeon inspects the area around the ileocolic vessels.
The surgeon continues to dissociate the superior mesenteric vein towards the head side, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon ligates and incises the root of the right colic artery, completing the dissection around the superior mesenteric vein.
The surgeon identifies the right colonic vein originating from the gastrocolic vein trunk, highlighting its anatomical variation in this patient.
The surgeon ligates and incises the root of the middle colic artery, completing the vascular control in the surgical field.
The surgeon ligates and incises the root of the accessory middle colic vein, completing the vascular dissection in the region.
The surgeon ligates the middle colic vein, securing it before incision.
The surgeon identifies the middle colonic vein flowing into the superior mesenteric vein, highlighting the vascular anatomy relevant to the procedure.
The surgeon ligates the colonic branch of the gastrocolic vein trunk, securing the vessel before incision.
The surgeon continues to dissociate the tissue gap to the right, progressing towards the hepatic flexure, with visible manipulation of the surrounding structures.
The surgeon dissociates the adhesion between the hepatic flexure and retroperitoneum from the surface of the genital fascia, carefully separating these structures.
The assistant elevates the iliopsoas muscle, allowing the surgeon to sharply dissect the lateral peritoneum around the paracolic sulcus of the ascending colon.
The surgeon completely resects the mesocolon of the red hemicolon, separating it from surrounding tissues.
Place the patient to the dorsal elevated position of twenty degree angle and tilt to the right by fifteen degree angle.
The assistant lifts the gastric body, providing exposure to the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon continues to dissociate the omentum medius levers, exposing the lower edge of the pancreas. The splenic flexure of the colon is completely dissociated along the pancreatic body and tail.
The surgeon dissociates the left toes gap along the lower edge of the pancreas, continuing the dissection in the region previously exposed.
The surgeon ligates and incises the inferior mesenteric vein, completing the vascular control step in the surgical field.

The assistant elevates the mesentery near the stump of the inferior mesenteric vein, while the surgeon incises the medial boundary of the left mesocolon, moving upwards and leftwards along the right side of the inferior mesenteric vein.
The surgeon continues to dissociate the toes gap leftwards, progressing towards the lateral peritoneum.
The surgeon separates the descending colon from the lateral peritoneum, completing the dissection in this area.
Place the patient in the Leo Davis position and tilt to the right by a fifteen degree angle.
The assistant lifts the sigmoid colon, providing better exposure for the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully dissociates the retroperitoneum from the right side of the sigmoid mesocolon, revealing the anatomical separation between these structures.
The surgeon enters the left toes gap along the right side of the inferior mesenteric artery, navigating through the surgical field.
The surgeon exposes the root of the inferior mesenteric artery, revealing its anatomical position and surrounding structures.
The surgeon ligates and incises the root of the inferior mesenteric artery, completing the vascular control at this anatomical site.
The surgeon completes the dissociation of the left toes gap, ensuring clear separation from the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully protects the left ureter and autonomic nerves, ensuring these structures are not damaged during the procedure.
The surgeon places gauze to protect the left ureter, gonad, and iliac vessels, ensuring these structures are shielded during the procedure.
The assistant pushes the sigmoid colon to the right, revealing the left mesentery for further dissection.
The surgeon incises the left white line of Toldt, separating the sigmoid colon and descending colon from the lateral peritoneum.

The surgeon associates the descending colon upwards and towards the pelvic entrance downwards, continuing the dissection and exposure of the colon in relation to the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon dissociates the posterior rectal space downward along the toe gap, continuing the dissection towards the pelvic diaphragm, with visible separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon separates the anterior rectal fascia from the posterior vaginal wall, ensuring the integrity of the posterior rectal mesangium during the dissection.
The surgeon completely dissociates the rectum by dividing the lateral rectal ligaments, ensuring clear separation from surrounding structures.
The surgeon resects the anus via the perineum and removes the specimen completely before proceeding to close the anal opening.
The surgeon uses absorbable barbed sutures to continuously close the pelvic peritoneum following pneumoperitoneum reconstruction.
The surgeon performs an ileostomy in the right lower quadrant, creating an opening in the abdominal wall to allow for the diversion of intestinal contents.
